title: CS50 for Business - Lecture 8 - Securing Systems
summary: CS50 for Business Lecture 8 covers securing systems through strong passwords, encryption, two-factor authentication, and cybersecurity best practices. The lecture is part of Harvard University's introduction to computer science and available through multiple platforms including edX and OpenCourseWare. The content emphasizes data protection strategies against cyber threats and hackers.
writeup: David Malan (CS50) opens a cybersecurity lecture by grounding security in two primitives: authentication (proving identity) and authorization (controlling what authenticated users can access). The talk then quantifies the weakness of common passwords using breach-derived top-10 lists, shows why character-substitution tricks like P@ssw0rd fail, and makes the abstract threat concrete by live-coding a Python brute-force cracker that exhausts all 10,000 four-digit numeric passcodes in under a second.
key_points: Authentication proves who a user is, while authorization is a separate primitive that governs which resources that authenticated user may actually access; both are necessary and neither alone is sufficient.

Breach analysis of leaked credential databases reveals that the most common real-world passwords include 123456, password, QWERTY, and multi-word phrases like iloveyou, all of which are trivially covered by dictionary and top-list attacks before any random guessing begins.

Character substitution heuristics, such as replacing A with @ or O with 0, are well-known to adversaries and are among the first transformations tried, so P@ssw0rd provides no meaningful additional security over the plain word.

A four-digit numeric passcode has exactly 10^4 (10,000) possible values, and a brute-force loop written in Python with itertools.product exhausts all of them nearly instantaneously, demonstrating that short numeric codes are trivially crackable with software.

The practical attack sequence is: try the top-100 or top-1000 list first, then run a dictionary attack over English words and common concatenations, and only then fall back to exhaustive enumeration, because probabilistic ordering maximizes the chance of an early match.
